title: TPU v1 · Power Signoff: Dynamic + Leakage, Peak + Average, within 28–40 W
oneLine: Power signoff computes both dynamic power—switching and short-circuit energy, approximately α·C·V²·f—and static leakage under real activity and worst corners. Post-route netlist/SPEF, characterized libraries, and VCD/SAIF or statistical activity yield peak power for supply integrity and average power for cooling/TDP. TPU's multi-Vt leakage savings and full-load array activity receive their final pre-silicon check here.

validates multi-Vt</text></g></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="438" y1="110" x2="492" y2="100" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><line class="cs-arr" x1="438" y1="165" x2="492" y2="175" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="peak" data-g="peak"><rect class="cs-box" x="496" y="62" width="204" height="70" rx="8" fill="#fff7ed" stroke="#fdba74"/><text class="cs-t" x="598" y="86">③ Peak power (transient)</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="106">Worst full-array switching window</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="124">→ supply integrity / IR spike</text></g><g class="cs-part" data-k="avg" data-g="avg"><rect class="cs-box" x="496" y="142" width="204" height="70" rx="8" fill="#f0fdf4" stroke="#86efac"/><text class="cs-t" x="598" y="166">③ Average power (thermal)</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="186">Integrated representative workload</text><text class="cs-sub" x="598" y="204">→ cooling / sustained TDP</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="598" y1="132" x2="598" y2="142"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="budget" data-g="budget"><rect class="cs-box" x="180" y="246" width="360" height="120" rx="8" fill="#fefce8" stroke="#fde047"/><text class="cs-t" x="360" y="270">④ TDP gate (28–40 W range; legend.name: ① Real inputs for power signoff
legend.desc: Use the post-route netlist plus SPEF for real capacitance, characterized .lib leakage/internal energy/pin capacitance, and switching activity from VCD/SAIF or statistical propagation. Evaluate MMMC corners because leakage rises sharply at hot, fast conditions. TPU's actual activity, SPEF, and corners are unpublished.

legend.name: Dynamic power (consumed by switching)
legend.desc: Charging/discharging load capacitance scales approximately as α·C·V²·f, plus short-circuit current during transitions. At 700 MHz with 65,536 MACs active, array switching can dominate. Weight-stationary reuse, regular dataflow, and idle-column gating reduce it; signoff quantifies the result.

legend.name: Leakage power (static)
legend.desc: Subthreshold and gate leakage flow even without switching. Leakage rises strongly with temperature and fast-process corners, creating thermal feedback. Multi-Vt uses HVT on relaxed paths and LVT only where timing requires; signoff measures whether that compromise controls worst-case leakage.

legend.name: ③ Peak power (transient supply integrity)
legend.desc: Peak power captures a worst short window such as simultaneous full-array switching. Its current pulse can create IR-drop and di/dt noise, so it must fit transient supply margin and becomes an input to EM/IR analysis. Scenarios and values are illustrative.

legend.name: ④ TDP gate (both peak and average must pass)
legend.desc: Average power over representative workloads determines sustained cooling/TDP; peak power determines transient supply integrity. Either can block release. TPU's disclosed 28–40 W range supplies context, while the 40 W line and plotted proportions are illustrative rather than a published breakdown.

steps.cap: ① Prepare the post-route netlist and SPEF, characterized power libraries, VCD/SAIF or statistical switching activity, and MMMC corners including worst hot/fast leakage.
② Compute P_total = dynamic switching/short-circuit power + static leakage using real activity; quantify the effect of multi-Vt leakage reduction.
③ Separate peak power from the worst full-array switching window and average power over representative workloads. Peak drives IR integrity; average drives thermal/TDP.
④ Release only if average power fits sustained cooling/TDP, peak power fits transient supply margin, and full-load operation remains in the illustrative 28–40 W budget band.

before: Use real post-route inputs: Power signoff requires the final gate netlist and SPEF capacitance/resistance, plus .lib leakage, internal energy, and pin capacitance. Pre-layout capacitance estimates are insufficient.

Choose traceable activity sources: Use representative functional VCD or compressed SAIF/SAF where possible; otherwise propagate statistical toggles. Define separate activity data for typical average workloads and worst full-array peak scenarios.

Define corners and criteria: Evaluate peak leakage at the highest junction temperature and fastest process, and representative average power at nominal conditions. Require average ≤ sustained TDP and peak ≤ transient supply margin; exact values are illustrative.

Align multi-Vt and low-power acceptance: Define leakage budget, acceptable LVT use, and whether gating or power domains are active in each power mode. Signoff measures whether these implementation choices deliver their promised savings.

Disclosure boundary: Dynamic/leakage decomposition, α·C·V²·f, VCD/SAIF, MMMC, peak versus average, TDP/IR criteria, and multi-Vt are standard practices. TPU's power breakdown, activity, corners, and Vt mix are not public.
during: Core rule: compute dynamic + leakage and inspect peak + average. Dynamic dominates active switching; leakage persists at idle and rises with temperature. Peak governs supply integrity; average governs cooling. Omitting any term leaves signoff incomplete.

Activity quality dominates accuracy. The same layout differs greatly between idle and full-array matrix multiply. Use reviewed, traceable typical and worst-case activity. Underestimating activity creates a false pass; gross overestimation causes unnecessary overdesign.
Sign leakage at the worst hot/fast corner. Temperature and fast process increase leakage and can reinforce heating. Confirm total power remains controlled at the maximum intended junction condition.

Feed peak current into power integrity. Simultaneous 256×256-array switching can create IR-drop spikes and di/dt noise. Pass the worst peak-current waveform to EM/IR signoff (§3.5), not merely compare it with TDP.
Validate the multi-Vt tradeoff. Measure leakage from LVT needed for critical paths. If budget is exceeded, replace relaxed LVT/SVT cells with HVT through ECO while preserving timing.

State methodology provenance: PrimePower/PowerPro/Voltus-class power analysis, activity files, MMMC, and the dynamic/leakage equations are standard signoff methods. TPU's 28–40 W and architecture provide context; all plots, scenarios, and thresholds are illustrative.

result.label: Dynamic power (example)
result.value: Dominant under full-array activity; reduced by weight-stationary dataflow and gating; average must fit TDP

result.label: Leakage power (example)
result.value: Evaluate at hottest/fastest corner; control with HVT on noncritical paths and avoid thermal runaway

result.label: Peak power → power integrity
result.value: Worst transient current ≤ supply margin; provide to §3.5 EM/IR analysis for IR-drop spikes

result.label: Average power → TDP/thermal
result.value: Long-term representative average ≤ sustainable cooling; TPU-class full-load context is 28–40 W

result.label: Release criterion (example)
result.value: Average ≤ TDP and peak ≤ transient margin and leakage ≤ budget

after: Return failures to ⑧ ECO and re-sign: Reduce average with HVT, downsizing, or gating; reduce peaks with decoupling, stronger PDN, or staggered activity. Rerun identical activity/corners and reconfirm timing.
Close the loop with EM/IR: Provide worst peak current to §3.5. If IR drop or EM fails, jointly strengthen the PDN or reduce peak activity and rerun both analyses.

Cross-check timing after power ECO: HVT, voltage reduction, gating, and power domains can slow paths or alter activity. Rerun STA at 700 MHz; neither timing nor power may be fixed by breaking the other.
Feed the overall tapeout gate: Power passes only when dynamic/leakage and peak/average are all within budget, alongside green STA, EM/IR, DRC/LVS, and LEC.

Compare predictions with silicon later: Measure full-load and temperature-dependent leakage after fabrication. Systematic error should refine future activity assumptions and libraries; no private TPU measurement is invented here.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the 65,536 8-bit MAC array, 700 MHz≈92 TOPS, 24 MiB Unified Buffer, 28 nm, die ≤331 mm², 28–40 W full-load range, and weight-stationary dataflow are published context. Dynamic/leakage models, α·C·V²·f, VCD/SAIF, MMMC, peak/average accounting, multi-Vt, TDP/IR criteria, and power tools are standard illustrative signoff practice, not a published TPU breakdown, activity set, corner, leakage share, or Vt mix.)
